message:
collapse startStream() into detect() overload

Single entry point for both batch and streaming. The function picks
behaviour from the type of its first argument:

  // Batch — pass a string, await the result
  const result = await filter.detect("Hi John, my email is x@y.com");
  result.spans;   // DetectedSpan[]

  // Streaming — pass an AsyncIterable<string>, get a handle that
  // exposes both the final result Promise and a span AsyncIterable
  const handle = filter.detect(llmTokenStream);
  for await (const span of handle.spanStream) { ... }
  const final = await handle.result;

Same model as Vercel `streamText`: the handle is sync-returned and
exposes parallel surfaces (Promise + AsyncIterable) backed by one
underlying job. The naming difference is by argument type, not by
function name.

Removed the `startStream()` method and the `DetectStreamSession`
interface — they were the second way to do the same thing. Renamed
`createDetectStream` → `streamDetect`, and `DetectStreamSession` →
`DetectStreamHandle`. Multi-consumer queue inside the streaming
driver lets `spanStream` and `result` both read from the same
underlying span emission stream without losing items.

stream.spec.ts updated to use the new shape; 6/6 spans still match
batch detect() output.

Docs (README, index.mdx, architecture.mdx, packages/textsift-core/
README.md) updated to show the overload pattern.

files.patch:
@@ -94,7 +94,7 @@ Thin wrapper around `AutoModelForTokenClassification`. We bypass `pipeline('toke
 
 ## Design invariants
 
-1. **Public API is narrow** — `PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`, `redactBatch`, `startStream`, `dispose`, plus the exported types.
+1. **Public API is narrow** — `PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect` (overloaded for both `string` and `AsyncIterable<string>`), `redactBatch`, `dispose`, plus the exported types.
 2. **Backends are interchangeable** — all backends produce byte-identical spans on the same input.
 3. **Weights live outside the npm bundle** — `textsift-core` ships 76 KB gzipped of JS + a 90 KB WASM module. The 770 MB model is fetched on first use and persisted via OPFS by default.
 4. **No server-side fallback** — if inference can't run locally, an error is thrown. The library does not call any server.

@@ -30,7 +30,7 @@ The model is OpenAI's; the value of this package is packaging:
 - Two custom backends — WGSL kernels for WebGPU and Zig-compiled SIMD WASM — that produce the same logits as transformers.js's WebGPU path on the same input.
 - A WebAssembly path that works at all on this model: ORT-Web's WASM EP (which transformers.js uses under the hood) is missing the `MatMulNBits` and `GatherBlockQuantized` kernels the int4 export needs, so `transformers.js` with `device: "wasm"` fails session creation. textsift's WASM backend is a from-scratch implementation that doesn't go through ORT-Web.
 - Persistent OPFS caching of the 770 MB model weights, configured by default. Same data the model needs anyway, just stored on the user's disk so the second visit doesn't re-download. transformers.js can be configured to do this too — by default it uses the Cache API which silently rejects payloads this big.
-- An incremental `startStream()` API for AI-proxy / LLM-output-filtering use cases. Streams text, yields spans as they become detectable, O(N) total work over a stream of N tokens.
+- A streaming overload of `detect()` — pass an `AsyncIterable<string>` (LLM token stream, ReadableStream-derived iterable, etc.) instead of a `string`. Yields spans as they become detectable. O(N) total work over a stream of N tokens, vs O(N²) for the naive "call detect after every chunk" pattern.
 
 ## Two packages

@@ -39,9 +39,12 @@ const result = await filter.redact(
 ```ts
 PrivacyFilter.create(opts?): Promise<PrivacyFilter>
 filter.redact(text, opts?): Promise<RedactResult>
-filter.detect(text, opts?): Promise<DetectResult>
+
+// detect() overloads on input type:
+filter.detect(text: string, opts?): Promise<DetectResult>          // batch
+filter.detect(input: AsyncIterable<string>, opts?): DetectStreamHandle  // streaming, O(N)
+
 filter.redactBatch(inputs, opts?): Promise<RedactResult[]>
-filter.startStream(opts?): Promise<DetectStreamSession>  // incremental, O(N)
 filter.dispose(): void
 
 // Standalone tokenizer (token counting, custom chunking, etc.)
